intro.lede: The Seatbelts were assembled in 1998 by composer and keyboardist Yoko Kanno for one purpose: to score Cowboy Bebop. A large, rotating big-band of session players, they made a soundtrack rooted in jazz but fearless about genre — bebop and big-band swing, blues, gospel, funk, fusion, Latin, country, electronica and full orchestra.
intro.voices: Featured voices include Mai Yamane (the gravelly blues of “The Real Folk Blues” and “Blue”), Steve Conte (English rock vocals), jazz singer Tulivu-Donna Cumberbatch, and “Gabriela Robin” — a pseudonym Kanno herself used for songs sung in an invented language, like “Green Bird” and “Cats on Mars.”
intro.disclaimer: A note on the chord guides below: these are simplified, beginner-friendly progressions chosen to teach the concept and capture the feel of each piece — not official transcriptions. The original arrangements are richer (extended chords, key changes, horn voicings). Verified facts (key, form, where it's used) come from the sources linked on each track; the “try this” progressions are teaching arrangements.

tracks.title: Tank!
tracks.vocalist: Instrumental (brief spoken intro by Tim Jensen)
tracks.usedIn: The title sequence of (almost) every episode — the “3, 2, 1, Let's Jam!” cold open.
tracks.genre: Big-band jazz over a 12-bar blues (a Latin-tinged hard-bop feel)
tracks.theory: Despite the show's name, “Tank!” isn't literal bebop — it's a big-band barn-burner built on the oldest form in the book: the 12-bar blues. Twelve bars, three chords (the i, iv and V of the key), repeating. Over it sit a walking double-bass line, bongos, brass stabs and a screaming alto-sax solo (Masato Honda). Learn the 12-bar blues and you've learned the skeleton of half the music in this show.
tracks.tryThis: Play one chord per bar, four beats each, with a hard swing. The last two bars (A♭7 → G7) are the “turnaround” that snaps you back to the top. Add a walking bass line (root–3rd–5th–6th) and you've got the engine of “Tank!”.

tracks.title: Rush
tracks.usedIn: Gunfights and pursuits across the series — including Spike's brawl at the bar in Session 1.
tracks.theory: This is where the show gets closest to actual bebop: breakneck swing, a full horn section trading fast lines, and the harmonic engine of jazz — the ii–V–I. That three-chord cell (minor-seventh → dominant-seventh → major-seventh) is the sentence-structure of jazz; bebop players race through it in every key, threading chromatic passing notes between the chords.
tracks.tryThis: Loop Dm7 → G7 → Cmaj7 as fast and swung as you can. Then move the whole cell to a new key every few bars (try down a whole step: Cm7 → F7 → B♭maj7). Stringing ii–V–I cells through shifting keys is the core move of bebop.

tracks.title: Space Lion
tracks.usedIn: Closes “Jupiter Jazz (Part 2)” — the eulogy for the saxophonist Gren. The longest cue on the OST (~7 min).
tracks.theory: Where “Rush” sprints through chords, “Space Lion” barely moves — that's modal jazz, the world of Miles Davis's “Kind of Blue.” Instead of a fast chord cycle, you sit on one chord (a “mode”) and let the melody breathe and explore. The whole feeling comes from restraint: a long, mournful sax line over an almost-static bed.
tracks.tryThis: Vamp on Dm7 and improvise using only the D Dorian scale (the white notes from D to D). Don't rush to change chord — let two or four bars pass. For a touch of color, drift to E♭maj7 and back. The space is the point.

tracks.title: Green Bird
tracks.usedIn: The slow-motion cathedral fall in “Ballad of Fallen Angels” (Session 5).
tracks.theory: A sharp left turn from the jazz: a hymn-like choral piece, soprano soaring over sparse accompaniment in a made-up tongue. Its grief comes from one of the oldest tricks in Western music — the “lament bass,” a bass line that walks slowly downward step by step. Ears hear a descending bass as sinking, mourning, falling — perfect for Spike's plunge from the window.
tracks.tryThis: Play these slowly, two beats of breath between each — notice the bass falling D → C → B♭ → A. That sinking line is the lament. Let a voice or top note hang above it. Solemn, sacred, in no hurry.

tracks.title: Waltz for Zizi
tracks.usedIn: Wistful beats throughout — Jet by the water in “Ganymede Elegy,” quiet character moments.
tracks.theory: A waltz simply means three beats to the bar instead of four — ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three. A “jazz waltz” keeps that lilt but dresses the chords in sevenths. It's gentle, nostalgic, and a lovely first taste of playing in triple meter.
tracks.tryThis: Count in threes and play one chord per bar — bass note on beat 1, the rest of the chord on beats 2 and 3. That ‘boom-chick-chick’ is the waltz feel. The Cmaj7 → Am7 → Dm7 → G7 loop circles gently back on itself.

tracks.title: Cats on Mars
tracks.usedIn: Radical Edward's leitmotif — the show's quirky, comic colour.
tracks.theory: The oddball of the soundtrack: bouncy synths and cute sing-song nonsense, deliberately ‘spacey’ and off-kilter to match Ed. A simple way to get that floating, slightly alien cheer is to slide between major chords a whole step apart — the ear can't quite find ‘home,’ which is exactly the woozy, weightless effect.
tracks.tryThis: Play bright, staccato major chords stepping up C → D → E and back. Because they're all a whole step apart, nothing feels like a fixed key — that's the spacey wobble. Keep it light and bouncy, almost like a toy.

tracks.title: The Real Folk Blues
tracks.composer: Yoko Kanno (lyrics: Yuho Iwasato) · The Seatbelts
tracks.usedIn: The end credits of most episodes — the show's emotional signature.
tracks.theory: The flip side of “Tank!”: same blues roots, opposite tempo. A slow, smoky ballad carried by Mai Yamane's weary voice over organ and electric guitar. A classic, soulful way to harmonize this kind of longing is the I–vi–ii–V loop — the ‘heartbreak’ changes behind countless ballads and doo-wop tunes.
tracks.tryThis: Play it slow and behind the beat, with a swung, bluesy lean. Same four chords as the waltz, but in 4/4 and dragged — let each one ring. Bend a few blue notes (the flat-3rd and flat-5th) in the melody on top and it weeps.
tracks.youtube: Cowboy Bebop The Real Folk Blues Mai Yamane

tracks.title: Blue
tracks.composer: Yoko Kanno (lyrics: Tim Jensen) · The Seatbelts
tracks.usedIn: Replaces the usual ending theme in the finale — Spike's funeral march as the camera pulls back to the stars.
tracks.theory: The grand finale: a wordless Latin chorus opens, then the song swells into a building, cathartic rock-gospel ballad. Gospel lifts a song by leaning on the bright I–IV–V chords and rising toward the IV — it feels like ascension, the right shape for a send-off. (The original sits in G♯ major; play it in friendlier G or A.)
tracks.tryThis: Start hushed on G, then open up as you reach for C (the IV) — that lift toward the IV chord is the gospel ‘rise.’ Build dynamics: begin almost whispered, end full and ringing. That swell is what carries Spike up to the stars.
tracks.youtube: Cowboy Bebop Blue ending Mai Yamane
